description: This figure presents a quantitative plot of density as a function of aluminum content for lightweight steel alloys, comparing experimental measurements against thermodynamic calculations. The graph displays two distinct experimental datasets: open triangles representing binary Fe-Al data compiled by Frommeyer and Drewes (2000), and open squares denoting quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn (wt.%) alloys evaluated by picnometry. A solid continuous line indicates Thermo-Calc density calculations for the quaternary system using CALPHAD modeling with volume data from commercial databases. The x-axis spans 5–13 wt.-% Al, while the y-axis ranges from 6.4 to 7.6 g/cm³. Both datasets demonstrate a linear decreasing trend in density with increasing aluminum content, reflecting aluminum's lower atomic mass relative to iron. The experimental data for quaternary alloys appears slightly lower than binary Fe-Al values, suggesting a possible manganese effect or inter-laboratory variation. The Thermo-Calc calculation agrees well with experimental measurements at lower aluminum contents but exhibits minor deviations at higher concentrations, potentially due to unmodeled kappa-phase precipitation. This density reduction (8–10% compared to conventional low-carbon steels) establishes aluminum addition as a critical design parameter for automotive lightweighting applications targeting CO₂ emission reductions.

Figure 2 presents isoplethal sections of the quaternary Fe–8Mn–0.2C (wt.%) system calculated with three distinct CALPHAD thermodynamic databases across 0–10 wt.% Al and 600–1400°C. Panel (a) utilizes the Hallstedt database incorporating Chin et al. assessment (PrecHiMn-01), displaying complex phase equilibria involving FCC (austenite), BCC (ferrite), Liquid, Cementite, M5C2 carbide, and κ-phase regions, with intricate low-temperature decomposition reactions below 800°C. Panel (b) employs the Umino et al. assessment (PrecHiMn-02), exhibiting simplified phase boundaries dominated by FCC, BCC+FCC, and BCC+FCC+κ fields but notably omitting M5C2 and Cementite stability regions. Panel (c) applies the Lindahl and Selleby assessment (PrecHiMn-03), predicting comparable high-temperature liquidus boundaries but differing significantly in the κ-phase precipitation domain below 700°C at Al contents exceeding 6 wt.%. These comparative calculations reveal substantial database-dependent variations in predicting phase stability, particularly regarding the competition between austenite, ferrite, and κ-phase at service temperatures, which fundamentally impacts microstructure design strategies for low-density automotive steels.

Figure 3 displays CALPHAD-calculated austenite (FCC) phase fraction as a function of temperature for an 8Mn-0.2C (wt.%) base alloy containing either 6 wt.% or 8 wt.% aluminum, comparing predictions from three distinct thermodynamic databases (PrecHiMn-01, PrecHiMn-02, and PrecHiMn-03). The x-axis spans temperatures from 600 to 1400°C, while the y-axis represents the FCC fraction ranging from 0 to 1.0. The plot reveals substantial discrepancies between databases: PrecHiMn-02 (dashed line) predicts significantly higher austenite stability with peak fractions approaching 0.75, whereas PrecHiMn-01 (grey line) shows considerably lower FCC stability. Solid lines denote 6 wt.% Al (thick) and 8 wt.% Al (thin), illustrating that increased aluminum content generally suppresses the FCC phase fraction. These computational results underscore critical uncertainties in phase stability predictions—particularly concerning the fcc+bcc+κ three-phase domain vital for continuous annealing—and emphasize the urgent need for experimental validation to reconcile thermodynamic assessments of Al-Mn-C systems.

Figure 4 presents an isothermal section of the Fe-Mn-Al-C system at 850°C with a fixed aluminum content of 8 wt.-%, illustrating the phase fields as functions of manganese (0–25 wt.-%) and carbon (0–1.0 wt.-%) concentrations. The diagram delineates distinct phase regions including single-phase bcc (ferrite), single-phase fcc (austenite), and the intermetallic κ-phase, along with their two-phase and three-phase mixtures (bcc+fcc, bcc+κ, fcc+κ, and bcc+fcc+κ). At low Mn and C contents, the bcc phase dominates, dissolving small amounts of carbon. Increasing carbon promotes κ-phase formation, requiring higher Mn contents to suppress it during continuous annealing. To achieve stable austenite (fcc) at this temperature, both high carbon and high manganese contents are necessary. This phase diagram provides critical thermodynamic guidance for designing three families of lightweight steels—ferritic, duplex (bcc+fcc), and austenitic—while avoiding undesirable κ-phase precipitation in high-Al automotive steel grades.

This figure presents comparative microstructures of three families of high-aluminum low-density steels designed for automotive lightweighting applications. Image (a) shows a single-phase ferritic microstructure with equiaxed grains characteristic of Fe-Al binary alloys, with a scale bar of 40 μm. Image (b) displays a two-phase austenite-ferrite microstructure captured via EBSD, where the austenite phase is highlighted in red and exhibits a banded morphology distributed within the ferrite matrix, with a scale bar of 50 μm. Image (c) illustrates a three-phase TRIPLEX microstructure (also EBSD) where ferrite is marked in blue and dispersed within an austenitic matrix, showing fine grain boundaries with a scale bar of 20 μm. The progression from single-phase ferrite to dual-phase and then to austenite-based TRIPLEX demonstrates the microstructural evolution achieved by adding carbon and manganese to stabilize austenite, supporting the classification of lightweight steel families based on phase constitution.

This figure presents a strain rate–temperature processing map that delineates the activation domain of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) in a ferritic Fe-8 wt.% Al steel. The plot compiles experimental data from hot torsion tests performed across a temperature range of 900°C to 1100°C and strain rates from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 10 s⁻¹. Green circular markers indicate processing conditions where DDRX is not activated, while red markers denote conditions where DDRX is activated. A dashed line approximately separates these two regimes, revealing that DDRX is favored at high temperatures and high strain rates, whereas continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) occurs at lower temperatures and lower strain rates. The vertical axis employs a logarithmic scale covering 0.01 to 100 s⁻¹. This map serves as a critical guide for optimizing thermomechanical processing windows, enabling control over microstructural evolution and grain refinement while avoiding undesirable surface defects like roping in lightweight automotive steels.

This figure illustrates the complex three-dimensional morphologies of κ-phase precipitation in a 2Mn-8Al-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steel using complementary microscopy techniques. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph (scale bar 20 μm) showing a non-lamellar eutectoid microstructure after continuous cooling from 1400°C at 10°C/s and quenching at 870°C, where the κ-phase precipitates at the interfaces between austenite (γ) and ferrite (α) matrix. Panel (b) presents a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image after deep etching (magnification 1.46 KX, scale bar 10 μm), revealing that the κ-phase grows preferentially along the long axis of austenite, forming blade-like structures. Panel (c) shows an SEM micrograph (magnification 2.00 KX, scale bar 10 μm) after isothermal treatment at 870°C for 15 minutes, demonstrating the complete decomposition of austenite and the formation of a distinctive cocoon-like κ-phase encapsulation structure. These images collectively capture the evolution of κ-phase from interface precipitation to fully enveloping the austenite, supporting the understanding of phase transformation kinetics in high-aluminum lightweight steels.

Time-resolved TEM investigation of nanoscale κ-phase precipitation in a 28.8Mn-9.4Al-0.92C (wt.%) austenitic steel during isothermal aging at 500°C. The figure comprises four panels (a-d) showing the evolution from initial solution-treated state through 64 h aging. Each panel presents paired data: selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of austenite along the [100]γ zone axis (top row) and corresponding dark-field micrographs using {100} superlattice reflections (bottom row). Panel (a) captures the initial quenched state and 2 min aging, showing diffuse superlattice spots indicating early-stage κ-phase ordering with weak imaging contrast. At 16 min (b), reflections sharpen and intensify, signaling increased precipitate volume fraction. After 4 h (c), discrete spherical precipitates smaller than 5 nm become distinctly visible. Panel (d) reveals that after 64 h, κ-phase particles exhibit cuboidal morphology (5-10 nm) and align along <100> austenite crystallographic directions. This sequence demonstrates the nucleation, growth, and coarsening of coherent κ-phase precipitates that provide precipitation strengthening in high-Al low-density steels.

Figure 9 presents TEM characterization of ferrite microstructure evolution in a 29Mn-9.6Al-0.56C (wt.%) low-density steel during aging at 550°C. The figure comprises selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns along the [011]α zone axis and corresponding dark-field images using the {200} reflection. Subfigure (a) shows the as-quenched sample, where extra superlattice reflections are visible alongside the fundamental spots: {111} spots indicate the presence of DO3 structure while {200} spots correspond to either DO3 or B2 structures, with intensity differences suggesting a mixture of B2- and DO3-ordered structures resulting from high aluminum content and partitioning. Subfigure (b) displays the sample after 1 h aging at 550°C, where the intensity difference between {111} and {200} reflections disappears, indicating transformation to a fully ordered DO3 structure. The dark-field images reveal the corresponding microstructural contrast changes associated with this ordering transition, demonstrating that thermal aging promotes the evolution from mixed B2/DO3 to fully DO3-ordered ferrite in high-aluminum low-density steels.

This line graph illustrates the influence of annealing temperature (750–1000°C), holding time (1 min, 136 sec, 5 min), and alloy composition on the tensile strength (TS) of a series of 8.5Mn-0.2C (wt.%) austenite-ferrite alloys. The plot contains nine distinct curves grouped by three alloy variants: 6Al (green symbols), 6Al+XSi (red symbols), and 7Al (blue symbols), where the numbers denote approximate aluminum content in wt.% and XSi indicates silicon addition. For each alloy, three annealing durations are compared. The y-axis shows tensile strength ranging from 630 to 830 MPa, while the x-axis represents annealing temperature. All curves exhibit a consistent downward trend: tensile strength decreases as annealing temperature increases, with the rate of decrease being steepest at lower temperatures (800–850°C) and gradually plateauing toward 1000°C. The 6Al+XSi alloy consistently displays the highest strength values (715–800 MPa), significantly outperforming both 6Al (650–740 MPa) and 7Al (680–760 MPa) across the entire temperature range. The 7Al alloy shows intermediate strength, while the base 6Al alloy exhibits the lowest values. At 1000°C, all compositions converge toward similar strength levels (~650–720 MPa), suggesting that microstructural differences diminish at high annealing temperatures. The holding time (1 min vs. 136 sec vs. 5 min) has a secondary but measurable effect, with longer times generally resulting in slightly lower strength at equivalent temperatures. This figure demonstrates that silicon addition and increased aluminum content both enhance tensile strength retention, particularly during low-temperature annealing, while high-temperature processing reduces these compositional advantages.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of an 8.5Mn-6Al-XSi-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steel annealed for 136 s at different temperatures. Panels (a) and (b) display band contrast (BC) images overlaid with austenite phase maps (red), while (c) and (d) show BC images combined with inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of ferrite. The left column [(a) and (c)] represents the 800°C condition, revealing incomplete recrystallization with pancake-shaped ferrite grains elongated along the rolling direction and significant orientation gradients. The right column [(b) and (d)] shows the 1000°C condition, exhibiting fully recrystallized, equiaxed ferrite grains with more homogeneous orientation distribution. The red regions in (a) and (b) indicate retained austenite, which appears more uniformly distributed at 1000°C. All images share a 10 μm scale bar. These microstructural differences demonstrate that increasing annealing temperature from 800°C to 1000°C promotes complete ferrite recrystallization and grain equiaxation, correlating with improved formability in these lightweight automotive steels.

This figure presents engineering stress–strain curves for a fully austenitic 0.92C-28.8Mn-9.4Al (wt.%) alloy, illustrating the pronounced effect of austenite grain size on tensile properties. Six distinct curves represent grain sizes ranging from 1.65 μm to 40 μm (specifically 1.65, 2.2, 8.4, 12.4, 21, and 40 μm), depicted through varying line styles including solid, dashed, and dotted patterns. The data reveals that finer grain sizes substantially increase both yield strength (from approximately 400 MPa to 700 MPa) and ultimate tensile strength (from ~750 MPa to ~960 MPa), obeying a Hall–Petch relationship with coefficients of 461 MPa·μm^{1/2} and 351 MPa·μm^{1/2}, respectively. Conversely, uniform elongation and total elongation increase with grain size, exhibiting a saturation effect when grain size exceeds 10 μm. The finest microstructure (1.65 μm) achieves the highest strength but fractures at ~45% elongation, whereas the coarsest (40 μm) shows the lowest strength yet superior ductility reaching ~60% elongation, demonstrating the classic strength-ductility trade-off critical for automotive lightweighting applications.

The image presents a transparent wireframe 3D model of an automotive body-in-white (BIW) structure, with the front longitudinal beams or crash absorption zones highlighted in solid blue. This schematic illustrates the targeted application locations for low-density Fe-Al-Mn-C steels in lightweight automotive engineering. The blue-highlighted components represent critical energy-absorbing structural elements—specifically the front side members (FSM)—where low-density steel grades can substitute conventional dual-phase steel (DP600) to achieve substantial weight reductions (21–33% according to the paper's LS-DYNA crash simulations) while maintaining crashworthiness under dynamic compression loads (EuroNCAP 40% offset frontal crash protocol). The visualization contextualizes the two lightweight steel families evaluated in the review: the austenite-ferrite alloy (7.4Al-8.2Mn-0.2C) and the fully austenitic grade (9.3Al-25.8Mn-0.96C). The figure supports the central thesis that high-aluminum low-density steels provide a viable metallurgical solution for third-generation automotive lightweighting applications, demonstrating where these materials can be structurally deployed to reduce vehicle mass without compromising mechanical performance or safety-critical energy absorption characteristics.

This figure displays a finite element model of an automotive body-in-white structure, rendered as a transparent wireframe mesh showing the vehicle chassis architecture with two specific structural components highlighted in solid red. Based on the paper's context regarding crashworthiness simulations of low-density steels, these highlighted regions represent critical structural members—likely the A-pillars or front side members (FSM)—that were subjected to dynamic compression analysis using LS-DYNA finite element software under EuroNCAP 40% offset front crash conditions. The visualization supports the evaluation of advanced high-aluminum low-density steel grades (specifically 7.4Al-8.2Mn-0.2C austenite-ferrite and 9.3Al-25.8Mn-0.96C fully austenitic compositions) as lightweight replacements for conventional DP600 dual-phase steel in automotive structural applications. The simulation results demonstrate that these low-density steel components can achieve substantial weight reductions of 21% to 33% while maintaining required crash performance standards, illustrating the potential of complex metallurgical design for automotive lightweighting strategies.
